metadata.title: Why a Plan That Reads Perfectly Can Still Delete Your Files
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Claude Cowork candidate 06. Source chapter: claude-cowork/chapters/05-plans-approvals-and-redirection.md. One mechanism: fluent prose and numbered steps describe a plan's form, not its safety — a step can be well-phrased and irreversible at the same time; the missing safety step is invisible in the format.
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL #1F6F5C = the safe/readable structure (steps 1-3); CRIMSON #BF3339 = the unsafe/irreversible steps (4-5) and what they do
metadata.note: Exclusions: NO plan-review checklist enumeration, NO four-responses-to-a-plan taxonomy, NO redirection-instruction three-part format, NO computer-use section, NO stop-conditions discussion.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Teardown register (voices/teardown/VOICE.md + CLAUDE-BRAND.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the Claude outro from AUTHOR.MD :: NikBearBrown
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: A six-step plan for organizing a grant files folder. Fluent language. Logical order. Kenji reads it in twenty seconds and clicks approve.

card.copy: Why a Plan That Reads Perfectly Can Still Delete Your Files

beats.narration_text: Three steps later: every file in the folder renamed without a backup. Twelve files deleted. Including one that cannot be replaced.
beats.scene_description: a formatted document showing a numbered plan — clean, professional-looking, like a polished proposal
beats.image_prompt: B02, close-up of a clean numbered list document on a desk, six steps formatted clearly, looks professional and organized, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, warm cream tones, no faces visible

beats.narration_text: A plan that is fluent, logically ordered, and cleanly numbered should be safe. Steps four and five are irreversible and unsequenced from any backup. Why doesn't the format tell you?

document.quote: A plan that is fluent, logically ordered, and cleanly numbered should be safe. Steps four and five are irreversible and unsequenced from any backup. Why doesn't the format tell you?

beats.narration_text: Here is the plan. Six steps. Each numbered. The language is clean. Nothing looks alarming.

viz.label: six numbered steps, all in TEAL/neutral — 1. Inventory 2. Identify duplicates 3. Naming convention 4. Apply convention 5. Remove duplicates 6. Manifest
viz.note: the plan as it reads — looks clean

beats.narration_text: Here is what steps four and five do. Step four renames every file — no backup first. Step five deletes what it calls duplicates. No dry run. No review.

viz.label: same six steps; steps 4 and 5 now highlighted CRIMSON with danger labels: '4. renames all — NO BACKUP' / '5. deletes — NO DRY RUN'

beats.narration_text: Fluent prose and numbered steps describe the plan's form. They do not describe its safety. A step can be well-phrased and irreversible at the same time.

viz.label: two columns: FORM (TEAL — fluent, numbered, logical) and SAFETY (CRIMSON — backup? dry run? review?); no arrow connecting them

beats.narration_text: The safety question is independent of coherence. 'Remove duplicates' can be grammatically perfect and organizationally catastrophic. The format cannot tell you.

document.quote: "Remove duplicates" can be grammatically perfect and organizationally catastrophic. The format cannot tell you.

beats.narration_text: What a safe plan includes — a backup before irreversible steps, a dry run before deletion, a review checkpoint — is orthogonal to what makes prose fluent. The missing step is invisible in the format.

viz.label: the six steps with a gap between 3 and 4 where 'BACKUP + DRY RUN' should be; TEAL shows what was there, CRIMSON shows the missing safety step gap

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] missing safety step invisible in format

beats.narration_text: The folder looks clean. The twelve deleted files do not.
beats.scene_description: a tidy organized folder view on a screen — looks successful — but the previous version showed more files
beats.image_prompt: B10, close-up of a computer screen showing a clean organized folder with fewer files than expected, looks complete but missing something, editorial collage style, desaturated newsprint treatment, cream and charcoal tones, no faces visible

beats.narration_text: Illustrative. Six steps. Step five reads 'remove duplicates and outdated drafts.' The deleted files include an earlier grant application version — different from the final, enough that a program officer may ask about it.

viz.label: TEAL step 5 text 'remove duplicates' → CRIMSON list of deleted files; one item highlighted 'grant-application-v1.docx — earlier version, not duplicate'; 'illustrative'
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE — the deleted irreplaceable file

beats.narration_text: The plan looked complete. It was not safe. The step that could not be undone was step five. Step five read exactly like the other five steps.

viz.label: six steps shown; steps 1-4 and 6 in TEAL; step 5 in CRIMSON with label 'cannot be undone'; all steps same format
viz.note: THE EXAMPLE continued — step 5 indistinguishable by format

beats.narration_text: A coherent plan is not a safe plan. The format does not know the difference.

document.quote: A coherent plan is not a safe plan. The format does not know the difference.

beats.narration_text: A coherent plan is not a safe plan.

card.copy: A coherent plan is not a safe plan.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Claude Cowork series.
